channel.title: Radio National Breakfast - Breakfast Wrap
channel.description: Filtered feed containing only Breakfast Wrap episodes from Radio National Breakfast
channel.itunes:author: ABC Radio National

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Treasurer defends backdown over CGT carve-outs
item.description:
The federal government has unveiled carve-outs to its proposed capital gains tax changes, following complaints from business groups and start-ups.

A 50% capital gains concession for small businesses will be expanded to all those with a turnover up to 10 million dollars, and there will also be carve outs for startups and discretionary testamentary trusts. So how is the federal Treasurer defending the change?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Hanson's speech slammed by critics
item.description:
Pauline Hanson gave one of the longest speeches heard at the National Press Club in Canberra yesterday — and certainly one of the most controversial.

The One Nation leader — riding high in recent polling — advocated for a "mono-cultural" society by restricting immigration, targeting what she called "radical Islam".

Today we hear from the Special Envoy to Combat Islamophobia, and from the President of the Human Rights Commission. The new One Nation MP for Farrer also shares his views, and Tom Crowley explains how the speech will be used politically by all sides. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Australia looks into sexual assault claims against Israeli forces
item.description:
The AFP is probing allegations that Israeli forces sexually assaulted Australian activists involved in a humanitarian flotilla last month, accusations denied by the Israeli Ambassador to Australia.

On the Breakfast Wrap you'll hear from a father of one of the activists making these accusations, who attended a meeting with the AFP and Foreign Minister on the allegations. And we'll put his comments to the Israeli Ambassador to Australia Hillel Newman. 

Then, comments from the Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson are drawing criticism from Labor. He made the comments during an interview today on Radio National Breakfast, so what has he actually said?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: How Israel could derail Trump's Iran deal
item.description:
The world is waiting for further details on the deal signed between the US and Iran to stop the war in its tracks, but why could Israel's continuing invasion of Lebanon derail the prospects for peace?

We'll hear from the former US Ambassador to Lebanon and find out what the Australian government's response is. 

Then, the UK is following in Australia's footsteps with their own teen social media ban, so how is Britain trying to go even further?

And you'll hear an exclusive interview with one of the crew of NASA's next Artemis mission. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Peace agreement signed between Iran and US
item.description:
US President Donald Trump says the United States and Iran have agreed to a ceasefire deal to end their war.While all the terms of the deal are yet to be announced, we know the Strait of Hormuz is set to be reopened.

We also hear about the President's UFC match on the White House lawn. 

We explore an attempt to limit Switzerland's population to 10 million people.

And Socceroos legends react to a historic opening match at the World Cup. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: How Belfast migrants have weathered a violent week
item.description:
We speak to a Belfast local and migrant about how his family has weathered violent anti-immigration protests in the city.

"With that smoke coming in and the explosions of my neighbour's house and cars being set alight, my kids came to me and said, Daddy, are we okay here?"

Listen to the powerful account from Anselme Shima .

Then, two British ministers quit Keir Starmer's government on principle over defence funding - we unpack a chaotic day in British politics. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: How is the Coalition handling Pauline Hanson?
item.description:
One Nation has had a lucrative day fundraising in Perth, off the back of bumper polls suggesting it is the nation's most popular political party. So, how is the Coalition handling One Nation, amid internal suggestions the two parties cooperate ahead of the next election?

Then, Foreign Minister Penny Wong discusses the situation in the Middle East amid continued strikes between Iran and the US.

And we head to Belfast as migrant communities weather a second day of violent anti-immigration protests.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Social media ban six months on
item.description:
It's been six months since Australia's world-leading social media ban for under-16s took effect - so how effective has the policy been?

Then, one of the world's biggest and most valuable artificial intelligence companies - Anthropic - this month made an extraordinary appeal to rivals to pause work on the technology.

And we cross to Belfast as violent anti-immigration protests engulf parts of the city.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: ABC boss faces questions
item.description:
ABC Managing Director Hugh Marks faces questions about the resignation of ABC News boss Justin Stevens, the discussion around Grace Tame's new ABC podcast series, and the comments from ABC Radio host Charlie Pickering.

Sally Sara also discusses the future of the broadcaster, and any potential concerns from the KPMG scandal. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Remembering Richard Scolyer
item.description:
He was a renowned pathologist and a pioneer in melanoma research. But through his brain cancer diagnosis, Professor Richard Scolyer took Australians on a journey of hope and tenacity.

The former Australian of the Year has died at the age of 59.

We hear from one of his old footy mates, ex-Tasmanian Premier Will Hodgman and the Governor General Sam Mostyn. 

And another of today's King's Birthday Honours recipients Fran Kelly drops in.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap Special: Scandal at KPMG
item.description:
What do you know about the scandal engulfing one of Australia's biggest consulting firms? 

We hear from a former insider and ex-whistleblower, the politician who brought this story to light, and the federal government about how its responding, after a whistleblower alleged that auditors at the firm misused confidential client documents.

Its an explosive story we've been covering in depth all week. This special episode guides you through whats happened, and explains why its so significant. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Scrutiny of aged care funding algorithm
item.description:
It's been revealed this week an algorithm - used to determine how much government funding older Australians should get to help them live at home - has caused dozens of mistakes.

We put questions to the Minister for Aged Care and Seniors.

Then, as the US flags new tariffs on Australia and Canberra continues to sell its revised second-hand sub deal with Washington, you'll hear from Australia's former Ambassador to the US and federal Treasurer Joe Hockey.

The BBC's Barbara Plett-Usher will fill us in on the political response in Kenya to the Ebola outbreak in the DRC and America's controversial plans for a quarantine centre.

And we'll explore the outrage in the UK around a murder, and body-cam footage showing a now under-fire police response. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Florida sues OpenAI
item.description:
Florida has become the first US state to sue emerging tech giant OpenAI over the design and safety of its chatbot, ChatGPT.

The legal action comes as Florida pursues a criminal investigation into whether ChatGPT played a part in a mass shooting at Florida State University last year,  which left two people dead.

Then Ukrainians are preparing for another night of bombardment following a deadly wave of attacks from Russia. 

And Matt Canavan discusses the government's move to second-hand submarines, and the future of the Nationals amid the rise of One Nation.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The community impact of the ISIS brides
item.description:
Members of Australia's Yazidi community have told the ABC they're deeply traumatised following the return of Australian women with links to Islamic State. 

The terrorist group enslaved Yazidi populations in Iraq and Syria for years, and there are now a few thousand Yazidis living in Australia.

Australia has become one of the latest countries to charge its own citizens over alleged slavery crimes committed during IS's brutal rule across parts of Syria and Iraq.

We hear from a global NGO representing Yazidi survivors.

Then, Peter Garrett explains why he's holding a public inquiry into AUKUS. 

And we hear from the new One Nation MP for Farrer.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Abbott responds to One Nation's poll surge
item.description:
According to the latest polls, Pauline Hanson is now the country's most popular political leader, leading the most popular political party, outpacing Labor and the Coaliition

So with his party under fire, we hear from the new president of the Liberal Party, Tony Abbott. 

Then, why is Israel across the international headlines today. 

And we unpack the move towards second-hand submarines that the federal government says will save money. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Government stands ground on tax shake-up
item.description:
The Federal Government is standing ground on its tax shake-up. 

Government front-bencher Tim Ayres joins Sally Sara, to outline why he backs re-casting the tax system.

Agenda-setting news coverage, breaking news and the most significant stories impacting the lives of all Australians.

The Friday political panel dissects the issue - and where it's headed next.

And following news the Commonwealth will launch legal action against chemicals manufacturer 3M, Independent Senator Lidia Thorpe calls for the lawsuit to examine the possible health impacts of the P-FAS chemical.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Tax legislation enters parliament
item.description: Coverage and analysis of national and international events.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Senate tension over the Anti-Corruption boss
item.description:
Outgoing National Anti-Corruption Commissioner Paul Brereton faced a grilling last night by senators in Canberra.

Mr Brereton resigned on Monday - dogged by questions around his handling of defence ties.

We hear from Greens Senator David Shoebridge after a heated exchange he had with Mr Brereton. 

Then, the government is unveiling what it's calling the biggest shake-up to employment services in decades - so what will they mean?

And could the Indian Ocean play a greater role in how Australia projects power in our region?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The plan to reserve gas for Australia
item.description:
The government has released a plan for a gas reservation policy that would force exporters to supply the domestic market with 20 per cent of their exports each year.

We'll hear from the federal resources minister and her Coalition counterpart. 

Then 11 Australian activists detained by Israel earlier this month while on a flotilla delivering aid to Gaza have now returned home, following a global outcry over their alleged treatment in custody. One of the activists share's their story with us. 

And the AFL world is mourning the death of ex-player and coach Neale Daniher, who's died at the age of 65 after a long battle with motor neurone disease. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Is a new political party on the horizon?
item.description:
Independent MP Zali Steggal has confirmed on Radio National Breakfast that talks are underway among so-called 'teal' independents about forming a new political party. 

We hear from the MP, and the former Liberal Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull about why he thinks it would be a good idea. 

Then we also get the latest on the US negotiations with Iran, and what the impact is as they drag on.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: PM under fire over DV royal commission comments
item.description:
The Minister for Social Services, Tanya Plibersek has defended the Prime Minister amid criticism of his comments on calls for a Royal Commission into domestic violence. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Working on the Ebola frontline
item.description:
As Ebola infection numbers climb in central Africa, we hear from an infectious diseases physician who's previously worked on the world's biggest ebola outbreak more than a decade ago. 

Then, we turn to a separate health matter in Australia, as the federal government announces millions for diphtheria support package.

And we continue asking what questions people have about the government's tax changes. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Why this sector is alarmed by the tax changes
item.description:
Start-up founders are calling on the federal government to rethink ending the 50% capital gains tax discount - over fears it will cripple the sector.

The newly proposed inflation indexation model - announced in the federal budget last week - has been called the most punitive tax treatment on start-up equity in the western world by some in the sector.

We hear from all sides on the debate, including the federal government and an independent long calling for tax reform. 

Then, we get the latest on the Diphtheria outbreak from South Australia. 

And we cross to north London as Arsenal take out the EPL. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Why this retailer is ditching their tobacco license
item.description:
While public health advocates and the tobacco industry argue over the merits of lowering the tobacco excise, small businesses are caught in the middle. We hear from one who says he won't renew his tobacco license.

Then we get an update on the growing Ebola outbreak in the DRC and Uganda. 

And we speak with a Liberal concerned about his party's stance on immigration. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.
